coredata.dc:title: A comparative study of parameter identification methods for equivalent circuit models for lithium-ion batteries and their application to state of health estimation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jinghua
Liu, Yixin
Kainz, Josef
coredata.dc:description: Accurate estimation of the battery state is a crucial requirement for advanced battery management systems (BMS). Model-based state estimation methods represent the most promising option to meet BMS requirements, where the equivalent circuit model (ECM) is an effective balance between modelling complexity and accuracy. ECM's accuracy is influenced by the combination of chosen model type and parameter identification method. In this paper, batteries are aged under various conditions. Both frequency and time domain measurements are performed on batteries in a variety of aging states. These measurements are employed for comparing all combinations of 7 existing models with 7 common identification methods. In addition, the accuracy of SOH models based on ECM parameters is investigated. The experimental results indicate that for frequency and time domain measurements, the same identification algorithm may exhibit distinct performances. Overall, PSO, GWO and LSQ are ideal candidates. Among them, PSO and GWO perform optimally in the frequency domain environment, while LSQ is superior in the time domain environment. Furthermore, this conclusion does not change with battery aging. Meanwhile, a simpler model structure is even beneficial for efficiently monitoring SOH when utilizing the aforementioned superior identification methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Equivalent circuit model
Parameter identification method
Comparative study
State of health

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of computation duration on identification accuracy
Comparison of metaheuristics and least squares methods
Dependence of battery model parameters on SOH and SOC
Comparison of SOH estimation accuracy based on ECM parameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Second-order (2RC) equivalent circuit model.
Second-order (2RC) equivalent circuit model with an additional hysteresis component.
The structure of model A.
The structure of model B.
The structure of model C.
The structure of model D.
The structure of model E.
Generalized flowchart for metaheuristic algorithms.
Experimental procedure.

(a) Aging process of training set cells. (b) Aging process of test set cells. (c) Example of shifting in EIS spectrum with SOH variation (cell #10). (d) Example of shifting in HPPC voltage response with SOH variation (cell #10, SOC 50 %).
Box plots to show the robustness of individual parameter identification algorithms for different models at varying SOH: based on measured data from cell #10 at 50 % SOC.
Performance of various parameter identification algorithms for varying SOH.
Performance of diverse parameter identification algorithms under a variety of SOCs.
Performance of diverse parameter identification algorithms under a variety of computation durations.
Comparison of the relative RMSE of numerous identification algorithms: LSQ as a benchmark.

Dependence of battery model parameters on SOH and SOC: Frequency domain,
R 0
.

Dependence of battery model parameters on SOH and SOC: Time domain,
R 0
.

SOH estimation accuracy by employing various ECM parameters as inputs: (a-c) Utilizing EIS measurements at 30 %, 50 %, and 70 % SOC, respectively. (d-f) Utilizing HPPC measurements at 10 %/20 %/30 %, 40 %/50 %/60 %, and 70 %/80 %/90 % SOC, respectively.
Basic circuit elements employed in ECM.
Upper and lower bounds for each parameter to be identified.
Aging conditions for cells belonging to the training set.
Aging conditions for cells belonging to the test set. New test conditions that differ from the test set are bolded and underlined.

Minimum number of identifications with 95 % probability: mark the worse values in each column (i.e. “worst value*0.8” to “worst value”) in red and the better values in each column (i.e. “best value” to “best value*1.1”) in green.

head.ce:title: A comparative study of parameter identification methods for equivalent circuit models for lithium-ion batteries and their application to state of health estimation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical University of Munich, Campus Straubing for Biotechnology and Sustainability, Energy technology, Schulgasse 16, 94315 Straubing, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Campus Straubing for Biotechnology and Sustainability, Energy technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Technical University of Munich, Campus Straubing for Biotechnology and Sustainability, Energy technology, Schulgasse 16, 94315 Straubing, Germany.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Campus Straubing for Biotechnology and Sustainability, Energy technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate estimation of the battery state is a crucial requirement for advanced battery management systems (BMS). Model-based state estimation methods represent the most promising option to meet BMS requirements, where the equivalent circuit model (ECM) is an effective balance between modelling complexity and accuracy. ECM's accuracy is influenced by the combination of chosen model type and parameter identification method. In this paper, batteries are aged under various conditions. Both frequency and time domain measurements are performed on batteries in a variety of aging states. These measurements are employed for comparing all combinations of 7 existing models with 7 common identification methods. In addition, the accuracy of SOH models based on ECM parameters is investigated. The experimental results indicate that for frequency and time domain measurements, the same identification algorithm may exhibit distinct performances. Overall, PSO, GWO and LSQ are ideal candidates. Among them, PSO and GWO perform optimally in the frequency domain environment, while LSQ is superior in the time domain environment. Furthermore, this conclusion does not change with battery aging. Meanwhile, a simpler model structure is even beneficial for efficiently monitoring SOH when utilizing the aforementioned superior identification methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In response to climate change and environmental protection, electric vehicles have gained popularity in recent years [1]. Lithium-ion batteries have been widely accepted as the core component of electric vehicles due to their high energy density, prolonged useful life, and excellent efficiency [2]. To ensure stable and reliable lithium-ion battery operation, an efficient battery management system (BMS) is essential [3]. The efficient operation of the BMS is primarily based on the accurate estimation of the battery state, which includes state of health (SOH), state of charge (SOC), state of function (SOF), etc. [4]. Correctly evaluating and characterizing batteries' behavior according to their current state allows for more effective management of the battery system. This results in improved operational performance over their entire useful life.

Accurate estimation of battery state has always been a problem researchers have attempted to solve. This is due to the fact that battery state cannot be directly measured and the remarkably nonlinear nature of batteries' electrochemical behavior [5]. Currently common approaches to state estimation of lithium-ion batteries tend to be categorized into two main groups: data-driven and model-based approaches [6]. Although the data-driven approach requires no specific information about electrochemical mechanisms, the process of extracting representative information from historical monitoring data entails challenges in terms of both the quality and quantity of data [7]. The model-based approach requires prior knowledge, but its reduced computational burden makes this approach show significant potential for implementation in BMS applications [8].

The equivalent circuit model (ECM) is a widely applied battery model, which uses a relatively simple structure, offers impressive execution speed and is able to simulate batteries' dynamic behavior reliably [9]. The measurements required for ECM parameter identification are typically categorized into two main types. These are time-domain measurements represented e.g. by the hybrid pulsed power test (HPPC), and frequency-domain measurements like e.g. electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [10]. ECMs for time-domain measurements consist of idealized circuit elements such as resistors, capacitors, and voltage sources [11]. ECMs frequently reported in published literatures are constructed with resistor-capacitor (RC) networks of different orders [12]. For further improving battery models' performance, ECMs incorporating hysteresis elements are also considered to be suitable for time-domain measurements [13]. ECMs suitable for frequency domain measurements are typically fractional order models (FOM). Such models also have various structures with numerous combinations of circuit elements. Their common features are the utilization of constant-phase elements (CPE) instead of ideal capacitors, and the inclusion of Warburg elements for simulating the diffusion process [14].

ECM accuracy is determined by two factors. The first factor is the model structure. Since underfitting may result from insufficient model parameters, whereas overfitting may result from excessive parameters [15], the right approach to accurately simulate battery behavior relies on selecting the most appropriate ECM structure for each measurement. For time-domain measurements, Lai et al. compared 11 ECMs and concluded that higher-order ECMs have an advantage in robustness, but first- and second-order RC models are the most appropriate choices for balancing accuracy and reliability [16]. Tran et al. verified the applicability of various ECMs for four common electrochemical types of lithium-ion batteries and demonstrated that the first-order RC model and the first-order RC model with hysteresis elements are optimal for different types of batteries, respectively [13]. For frequency-domain measurements, Lurilli et al. discussed in detail the performance of various popular FOMs and presented a general strategy for selection of circuit elements reflecting different ranges of the EIS spectrum [17].

The second factor that determines ECM accuracy is the algorithm employed for parameter identification. The commonly applied algorithms fit into two main categories: deterministic methods based on least squares [18], and metaheuristics inspired by natural phenomena [19]. For deterministic methods, the method can obtain the optimal solution at a relatively low computation cost and with identical results for each computation. However, the method is extremely dependent on the initial estimate. It is prone to falling into a local optimal solution if the initial estimate is unfavorably chosen [20]. In order to solve the problems inherent to deterministic methods, metaheuristic algorithms have gained much attention in recent years. They have been incorporated into engineering optimization [21]. Despite the fact that such methods cannot guarantee identical results for each computation, they effectively avoid falling into local optimal solutions. For parameter identification in battery models, metaheuristic algorithms have gained considerable popularity. Examples include, but are not limited to: particle swarm optimization (PSO) [22], genetic algorithm (GA) [23], whale optimization algorithm (WOA) [24], etc.

Both the model structure and the algorithms employed for parameter identification have been extensively researched in the existing literature. However, previous studies usually focused on an individual one of the two factors. According to the no free lunch theorem, there exists no universal algorithm that solves every optimization problem [25]. This indicates that the optimal model structure and parameter identification algorithm may be different for specific measurement environments. Therefore, it is beneficial to investigate the performance of various combinations of model structures and algorithms in a variety of environments. Using fresh cells for time-domain measurements, Lai et al. extensively discussed the optimal combinations. They proposed relatively optimal parameter identification algorithms for several SOC regions and multiple model structures [26]. The ECM's performance, however, will be significantly affected by batteries' aging behavior [10]. [27–29] have reported significant variations in model parameters with increasing aging. As a result, various model structures and algorithms can produce varying ECM accuracy based on a certain SOH. This further affects ECM-based SOH estimation accuracy.

In summary, the purpose of this paper is to identify the optimal combination of model structure and parameter identification algorithms for multiple measurement environments and variations in battery states. Moreover, the study intends to find an existing ECM parameter identification method with relatively effective generalization performance. Accordingly, this paper employed both fixed and mixed aging conditions for aging tests. In addition, time-domain (i.e., HPPC) and frequency-domain (i.e., EIS) measurements were conducted under varying SOHs in several SOC regions, respectively. Five model structures were selected for EIS measurements and two for HPPC measurements. Seven representative optimization algorithms were employed to identify ECM parameters. The individual model structures and parameter identification algorithms are extensively discussed from the perspectives of stability, model accuracy at equal computation duration as well as the effect of different computation durations on the algorithms, and the performance of SOH estimation based on ECM parameters, etc. The results can provide a benchmark for the battery state estimation function module in BMS.

The remaining sections of this paper are organized as follows: Section 2 provides the technical background of this paper and Section 3 details the experimental arrangements. Section 4 presents a comparative discussion of the identification results from different perspectives, while Section 5 contains the conclusion and outlook.

The ECMs employed nowadays require the utilization of various combinations of basic circuit elements to simulate batteries' behavior. A variety of typical basic circuit elements have been proposed to represent electrochemical reactions and physical phenomena. The elements discussed in this paper are listed in Table 1 . For both time-domain and frequency-domain measurements, distinct ECMs are selected to achieve an appropriate balance between modelling accuracy and complexity. In this paper, a representative variety of ECM structures are investigated for each of these two different measurement methods. This is to make this work's conclusions more relevant to practical applications.

The strengths and weaknesses of various model structures applicable to time-domain measurements in terms of accuracy, stability, and robustness have been extensively discussed [16,30]. The conclusions largely endorsed by the academic community include:
-
Second-order ECM with two RC networks is a superior compromise between parameterization complexity and model accuracy [31], with such an ECM demonstrating satisfactory accuracy in both constant and dynamic current profiles [32].
-
For specific electrochemical types of lithium-ion batteries (especially LFP batteries), ECMs with hysteresis elements are considered a more suitable choice than conventional ECMs containing only RC networks [33].

Based on this, the 2RC and 2RCwH (2RC with hysteresis) models were chosen to discuss the performance of parameter identification algorithms in the time-domain measurement environment. For frequency-domain measurements, FOMs demonstrate superiority over traditional RC models, although parameter identification complexity is exacerbated by CPE components [34]. Nonetheless, the optimal model structure cannot be readily determined as more complex FOMs cannot always provide the most accurate simulation accuracy [35]. In this paper, five representative FOMs are selected to discuss parameter identification methods in a frequency-domain measurement environment.

A total of two ECMs suitable for time-domain measurements are employed in this work to compare parameter identification algorithms. One of them is the 2RC model as shown in Fig. 1 . It consists of an open-circuit voltage component OCV, an internal resistance
R 0
, polarization resistors
R 1
and
R 2
, and effective capacitors
C 1
and
C 2
. To facilitate parameter identification, the 2RC model equations can be expressed in discrete form as follows:
(1)

Where
U t
is the terminal voltage,
U oc
represents the open circuit voltage, I is the current and
R 0
refers to the internal ohmic resistance. U

C
2 are the time constants of those RC networks.

Hysteresis is a critical variable for specific battery technologies. Its value depends on three battery processes: charging, discharging, and charging/discharging transients. Hysteresis physics has not been sufficiently explained. However, domain theory relates hysteresis to separate regions inside the electrodes, leading to different electrode equilibrium potentials during charging and discharging [36]. Moreover, the hysteresis phenomenon alters the battery model's parameters, affecting its dynamic response [37]. Considering the above discussion, a 2RC model with single-stage hysteresis was investigated in this work, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Compared to the 2RC model, an additional component H can be incorporated into the ECM to represent the hysteresis effect:
(4)
U
t , i
=
U oc
−
R 0
I i
−
U
1 , i
−
U
2 . i
+
H i
(5)
H
i + 1
=
e
−
k
I i
∆ t
H i
± h
1 −
e
−
k
I i
∆ t
Where k is the hysteresis decaying factor, h is the maximum hysteresis voltage that is positive for charge and negative for discharge. “ ± ” represent charging and discharging, respectively.
In order to comprehend the various ECM structures more thoroughly, the basic circuit elements are discussed initially. Table 1 gives an overview.

Here, empirical constants Q (calledthe CPE capacitance), α < 1 and the Warburg coefficient σ are defined. Different combinations of these basic circuit elements constitute common ECMs applicable to lithium-ion batteries.

Through the elements listed in Table 1, five distinct ECMs, designated as Model A to Model E, can be applied to represent battery dynamic behaviors. Those ECMs may consist of ohmic resistance (
R 0
), resistance (
R sei
) and capacitance (
CPE sei
) of the interfacial layer, charge-transfer resistance (
R ct
), double-layer capacitance (
CPE ct
), and Warburg element (W).
R 0
represents the battery's internal resistance, which does not fluctuate significantly with the SOC but increases during cycling due to the depletion of the electrolyte and micro-crack formation within the particles.
R sei
represents the resistance that forms on the interface between the electrode and electrolyte. This interfacial layer is termed the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, which is generated by the decomposition of the electrolyte.
R ct
is associated with the dynamics of an electrochemical process and can be influenced by factors such as surface coating, phase transitions, bandgap structure, and particle size [38].

As the most prevalent second-order ECM with CPE, model A [39] is illustrated in Fig. 3 . Its impedance can be expressed as:
(6)
Z A
=
Z
R 0
+
Z
CPE , sei
Z
R , sei
Z
CPE , sei
+
Z
R , sei
+
Z
CPE , ct
Z
R , ct
Z
CPE , ct
+
Z
R , ct

Model B [40] and model C [41] are more complicated by integrating a Warburg element than Model A. They are illustrated in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 . As mentioned in [39], the Warburg element represents batteries' behavior in the low frequency region. The impedance of model B and model C can be written as follows:
(7)

Z W

On the basis of the above models, an inductor with various connections has been investigated in model D [42] and model E [43], as depicted below in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 . Regarding Model E, the parallel structure of resistor and inductor is mainly intended to reflect battery behavior in the high-frequency region [44]. The impedance of model D and model E can be formulated as follows:
(9)
Z D

R 0

R , ct

Z W

With the exception of the traditional deterministic approach based on least squares, all metaheuristic approaches to parameter identification share an essentially similar process based on varying specifics. Fig. 8 presents a generic flowchart representing metaheuristic algorithms. The process can be broken down into three distinct phases [45]:
-
Phase 1: Population and iteration termination condition initialization. Population initialization should be done by arbitrary selection of candidate solutions or mathematical expressions while observing upper and lower bounds. The fitness values of the candidate solutions will be computed via an objective function and sequentially ranked.
-
Phase 2: New candidate solutions are generated utilizing an algorithmic mechanism or update function. The fitness values of all candidate solutions will further be revised, candidate solutions will be ranked again accordingly.
-
Phase 3: Determine the candidate solution with the most suitable fitness value and check the termination criteria.

Although metaheuristics have similar basic ideas, various algorithms developed from different natural phenomena show individual benefits and drawbacks. In this paper, six metaheuristic algorithms and one deterministic algorithm that are frequently employed and have demonstrated clear potential in existing research are selected for comparison. The algorithms to be compared are listed as follows:
-
Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm (LSQ, chosen as an exemplary least-squares method) [46].
-
Firefly algorithm (FA) [47].
-
Genetic algorithm (GA) [48].
-
Grey wolf optimization (GWO) [49].
-
Particle swarm optimization (PSO) [50].
-
Simulated annealing algorithm (SA) [51].
-
Whale optimization algorithm (WOA) [52].

Both metaheuristic algorithms and least squares methods, the upper and lower bounds of the identified parameters are critical factors affecting the algorithm's performance. In addition, the battery model parameterization requires that all obtained parameters have valid physical meanings. To achieve that, physically meaningful bounds (e.g. nonnegative resistances, etc.) for the parameters were chosen. To ensures that comparisons between various algorithms are fair and that battery parameters always remain within the feasible range, the same parameter ranges were used for all algorithms.
The upper and lower bounds for all the parameters identified in this work are presented in Table 2 .

In this paper, commercially available Panasonic NCR18650GA lithium-ion batteries of the same batch were employed for the experiments. This is an NCA battery with a rated capacity of 3.35 Ah and an operating voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V. All charge/discharge measurements were performed through a CTS system manufactured by BaSyTec, which provides a resolution of 0.3 mV and an accuracy of 1 mV. EIS measurements were conducted with a Gamry Interface 5000p system. To ensure the consistency of the experimental data, all measurements were performed in a climate chamber at a constant 25 °C.

To provide the comparison of parameter identification algorithms under multiple SOHs, as well as to further discuss the impact of various algorithms on SOH estimation accuracy, 14 cells were collected for measurements in this paper. Under the premise of carefully considering the significant influence of aging conditions on the degradation mechanism, the batteries are classified into a group of batteries where the aging conditions of the same battery are fixed (9 batteries in total), and another group where the same battery undergoes hybrid conditions (5 batteries in total). To evaluate the performance of parameter identification algorithms for various SOH conditions, followed by SOH estimation based on ECM parameters, corresponding cycle life tests are designed in this paper. This is similar to our previous classification [53]. Since the measurement environment of the second group was closer to the actual application conditions, they were named “the test set” (cell #10 - #14) based on their role in SOH estimation. The first group was named “the training set” (cell #1 - #9) accordingly. After these batteries reach various states of degradation, corresponding HPPC and EIS measurements are performed to obtain the measurement data required to identify ECM parameters.

During the cycle life tests, the constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) method was employed for all charge and discharge processes. Since the discharge rate does not significantly influence the degradation behavior of the NCA batteries investigated in this paper [54], a 1C discharge rate was applied to all batteries. Table 3 summarizes the aging conditions for cells belonging to the training set. These conditions are maintained throughout the test period. A variety of aging conditions results in diverse degradation mechanisms. To comprehensively demonstrate the difference performance of parameter identification algorithms under various degradation mechanisms, nine cells are included in the training set. These cells are subjected to cyclic aging at three different charging rates and three levels of DoD. It is worth mentioning that the DoD in this paper refers to the depth of discharge after charging the cell to 100 % SOC. This allows representation of diverse aging conditions at constant temperature. All cells are evaluated by HPPC measurements and EIS measurements after the same equivalent cycles (depending on the batteries' aging state: every 42 cycles or every 84 cycles).

Table 4 details the aging conditions for the five cells belonging to the test set. Combined aging conditions create more complex degradation mechanisms. To further compare the parameter identification algorithms under these circumstances, as well as to more accurately obtain close-to-realistic test set data for SOH estimation, hybrid tests were performed utilizing separate cells independent of the training set. Cell #10 was subjected to HPPC measurements and EIS measurements after every 42 equivalent cycles (except for 1C/100 % DoD, which results in excessive degradation). In contrast to the training set, the corresponding aging conditions fluctuate dynamically. In accordance with the cell #10's test conditions, cells #11, #12, and #13 were further modified with revised charge rate, altered DoD, and adjusted equivalent cycle number. These modified conditions did never appear in the training set. The revised parameters are shown in bold and underlined in Table 4. Compared to the training set, the test plan for Cell #14 adjusts all aging conditions to alternative values. These modified parameter settings are not only expected to validate parameter identification algorithms. Furthermore, they will investigate the generalization ability and reproducibility of the SOH estimation method. Thus, the performance of ECM parameter estimation using various identification algorithms is implicitly evaluated during SOH estimation.

The experimental flow for each cell consists of five parts: preparation, aging test, capacity test, HPPC tests, and EIS tests. Fig. 9 illustrates the experimental steps, a one hour relaxation period is performed between experimental steps:
Step 1: Perform three complete cycles in CC-CV mode applying 0.2C current. This will generate a stable SEI layer for each new cell and provide uniform initial conditions.
Step 2: Perform aging tests on different cells according to Table 3 and Table 4.
Step 3: The RPT (reference performance test) begins with a capacity test. It is performed in CC-CV mode using 0.2C discharge current, terminating when the voltage reaches 2.5 V and the current is <0.05C. The capacity value obtained that way will be applied to determine the battery's SOH.
Step 4: 11 HPPC tests (negative pulses followed by positive pulses) were performed. Those were repeated for 5 % ΔSOC intervals between 5 % and 20 % SOC and 10 % ΔSOC intervals between 20 % and 90 % SOC. Based on the general HPPC profile selection guidelines [55], the negative pulse height was selected to be 1.25C, the positive pulse height to be 1C, and the pulse length to be 5 s. After the positive pulse, the relaxation period lasted 30 min.
Step 5: As the final part of the RPT, EIS tests were performed for 30 %/50 %/70 % SOC in the frequency range of 10 mHz - 20 kHz, respectively.

Continue with Step 2 unless the 80 % SOH limit is reached. The HPPC and EIS measurements data will be utilized for ECM parameter identification. This will complete the comparison of parameter identification algorithms.

For a comparative analysis of the identification algorithms' performance during the entire battery lifetime, measurements according to the test plan in Section 3 were performed. The degradation trajectories of the training set and test set cells are illustrated in Fig. 10a and b, respectively. During the cyclic aging experiments, 14 cells were subjected to RPT at 205 unique SOHs containing the initial state. Accordingly, 615 EIS measurements and 2255 HPPC measurements were executed throughout the measurement period. These data obtained are applied to determine ECM parameters. Fig. 10c and d demonstrate the shifting characteristics of the EIS spectrum and the HPPC diagram during the entire aging process. Cell #10 is presented as a case study.

All computations were performed utilizing Matlab on a PC with 16 GB RAM and a 4.90 GHz processor. To facilitate an equitable comparison of the algorithms, the computational duration of all the metaheuristic algorithms was equalized to 3 s by setting the maximum number of iterations accordingly. The identical computation duration enables a comparison of the efficiencies of the different algorithms. The duration limit further avoids excessive computational resource consumption, thus accurately reflecting the algorithms' capability for real-world applications. In addition, all metaheuristic algorithms employ the same boundaries for parameters.

In this work, identification accuracy is always expressed by the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) between the simulated and measured values. However, for EIS measurements, RMSE evaluates the difference between simulated and measured impedance whereas for HPPC it represents the deviation between the simulated and measured voltage response.

The robustness of an identification algorithm influences its potential for real-world applications. Therefore evaluating an algorithm's ability to consistently provide reliable accuracy is crucial for discussing its performance. Since the hybrid aging conditions experienced by cell #10 are most comparable to real world application conditions, this paper examines its measurements over its entire lifespan. The purpose is to evaluate the robustness and reliability of individual algorithms. 27 EIS measurements conducted at 9 unique SOHs are employed to identify ECM parameters. With increasing model complexity it becomes more challenging to do robust identification, and more data are required. The EIS measurements deliver a significantly reduced amount of data compared to HPPC, which provides a clearer indication of the algorithm's robustness. The parameter identification was repeated 10 times for every combination of algorithm and model at each SOH and SOC. With consideration of the initial state, 10,500 parameter identifications were completed in this section.

Fig. 11 utilizes box plots to illustrate the robustness of numerous combinations at 50 % SOC and 9 different SOHs. The results at 30 % and 70 % SOC are presented in Figs. A.1 and A.2 and show similar pattern regarding the robustness. The convention chosen for the box plots is such that the top edge of the box represents the upper quartile of the RMSEs and the bottom edge represents the lower quartile. Outliers are marked with circles and are defined as deviations from the median >1.5 times the difference between the upper and lower quartiles. As a result, large boxes and numerous circles indicate insufficient robustness.

There is a consistent trend that the accuracy and robustness of model identification usually declines as SOH decreases. This could be the result of the fact that degradation aggravates the batteries' nonlinearity, resulting in challenges with parameter identification. However, individual algorithms still show different performance: FA and PSO maintain excellent robustness generally, while GA exhibits insufficient accuracy and stability. The remaining three algorithms exhibit relatively homogeneous behavior, all providing improved robustness under complex models (Model D and E), while suffering increased fluctuations under simplistic models. In order to corroborate these conclusions in a more quantitative perspective through model accuracy's robustness, the expected minimum number of identifications x required to obtain an RMSE of less than u with 95 % probability was calculated using the following formula [26]:
(11)
1 −
1 −
n m
x
= 0.95
where u is selected as the average measurement error of 0.263 mΩ, determined by the EIS measurement accuracy of the Gamry potentiostat (0.5 %–1 %) and the impedance range of the mid-frequency region (25 mΩ-45 mΩ). m = 300 is the total number of identifications, which result from 10 repetitions each for the 10 separate SOHs and 3 unique SOCs. n is the number of identifications for which the RMSE is less than u . A lower number for x corresponds to higher robustness. Table 5 presents results for x for the LSQ and metaheuristic algorithms. As noted in Table 5, the results can quantitatively provide insights that corroborate the boxplot conclusions described above. In addition, metaheuristic algorithms are generally acceptably robust for frequency domain models. For EIS measurements they outperform least squares methods.

The applicability of algorithms to various SOHs is a crucial indication of performance. All measurements of 14 cells are taken into account to discuss SOH's impact on the algorithm's performance. For EIS and HPPC measurements at multiple SOCs and SOHs, 53,095 identifications are performed in this section.

Fig. 12 illustrates the performance of various combinations at numerous SOHs. In order to concisely express the conclusions underlying vast amounts of data, the data points in Fig. 12 are obtained by curve fitting. The average RMSE for various SOC conditions at each SOH is presented in Fig. 12. With the exception of completely new cells, identification accuracy deteriorates with degradation for all combinations for EIS measurements. This is especially evident in complex models. The accuracy advantage of the complex model for fresh cells diminishes as SOH drops to 90 %. It disappears completely as the cell approaches end of life. For numerous modelling approaches, LSQ, GA and SA are significantly less accurate than the other algorithms. WOA provides relatively decent accuracy, but remains inferior to the lead group consisting of FA, GWO and PSO. It is worth mentioning that accuracy for brand new cells is frequently insufficient, which might be caused by nonlinearities in the cell's behavior caused by incomplete stabilization of the SEI (solid electrolyte interphase). However, the combination of FA, GWO or PSO with complex models can minimize this problem.

For HPPC measurements, the two models exhibit distinctive patterns. The RC models' accuracy varies with SOH analogously to the FOMs, deteriorating as SOH decreases. However, the hysteresis model is insensitive to SOH variations (except when implementing PSO). Therefore, while the 2RC model is slightly advantageous early in a cell's lifespan, the hysteresis model is instead quite beneficial in the final stages. As opposed to EIS measurements, LSQ and GA instead deliver a more desirable performance for the time-domain model, while SA's accuracy is limited. FA also appears to be an unwise alternatives then. However, PSO and GWO's performance in achieving optimal accuracy in both frequency and time domain measurement environments makes them superior candidates with generalization ability. Since each EIS measurement contains merely 65 data points, this amounts to a mere fraction of HPPC's data. The accuracy discrepancy between various algorithms is also a result of their differing generalization performance when applied to large or small data volumes.
Since EIS and HPPC measurements are conducted at various SOHs and SOCs, a different viewpoint on the results can be obtained by investigating the impact of SOC.

Fig. 13 illustrates the variation in fitting accuracy for multiple combinations at distinct SOCs over the entire lifespan. For HPPC measurements, SOC has a substantial effect. In the low SOC region (<20 %), the model accuracy drops dramatically. The error value becomes several times greater than in the high SOC region. However, since all algorithms show a similar behavior, the reduction in accuracy occurs can be attributed to the model characteristics being no longer well adapted to the system. Cell nonlinearity in the low SOC region is not suitable to be entirely expressed through RC networks. In the high SOC region, the hysteresis model exhibits less variability in accuracy, with all algorithms providing relatively consistent accuracy, the 2RC model's accuracy has decreased marginally at <50 % SOC as well as significantly at <20 % SOC.

Comparable to HPPC measurements, all of the models employed to EIS measurements are insensitive to SOC fluctuations in 30 %–70 % region. Due to the limitations of the measurement data in this work, it is impractical to discuss curve trends for <20 % SOC. However, at 30 %–70 % SOC, the curves barely cross as the SOC varies and we expect that the most suitable algorithms for that range will perform well for the full SOC range. As a result, for practical applications it is then sufficient to choose a single algorithm for the full SOC range. Algorithms with excellent accuracy such as PSO can be expected to provide reliable results regardless of SOC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of computation duration on identification accuracy
ce:section.ce:para: The discussion above is predicated on the premise that the computation duration is held constant at 3 s. For metaheuristic algorithms, the computation duration may crucially affect accuracy. In this section, 30 EIS measurements over the full lifespan of representative cell #10 are selected. Then, a shortened computation duration of 1.5 s and a lengthened one of 10 s are considered as alternatives. To this end, an additional 2700 identifications are utilized for the discussion of computation duration.

Fig. 14 illustrates the accuracy variation with SOH for numerous combinations employed in different computation duration conditions. For all combinations, the increase in computation duration leads to accuracy improvement. Nevertheless, the extent of this improvement is remarkably different. PSO exhibits the most impressive convergence rate. Even for the most complex model structures, an increase in computation duration does not result in a significant improvement in accuracy. FA provides an excellent convergence rate with fewer parameters. However, with more parameters the 1.5 s computation duration gradually loses accuracy compared to 3 s. The performance of GWO and WOA is comparable for differing model structures: the accuracy improvement of prolonged computation durations is substantial. Although, this difference is most apparent between 1.5 s and longer durations. A comparison between 3 s and 10 s demonstrates that the difference in accuracy is not proportional to the variation in duration. Meanwhile, the accuracy of SA and GA continues to increase with rising computation duration. Conversely, this means that long computation times are required to obtain good results which makes these algorithms insuitable for BMS.

Overall, FA, PSO, GWO and WOA exhibit exemplary performance at the computational duration level of 3 s. An acceptable balance of accuracy and computational resources is maintained by these algorithms in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of metaheuristics and least squares methods
ce:section.ce:para:
In currently available research, least squares-based algorithms are regarded as conventional algorithms for parameter identification. In order to more accurately represent the individual metaheuristic algorithms and their strengths and weaknesses, a relative RMSE has been defined as an indicator to quantitatively express the relative outperformance compared to LSQ:
(12)
relative RMSE i
=
average RMSE LSQ
−
average RMSE i
average RMSE LSQ
where i represents one of the 6 metaheuristic algorithms discussed in this paper.
average RMSE i
is the average of RMSEs for numerous SOHs and SOCs. Such a representation facilitates visualization of algorithms' performance. A positive relative RMSE indicates that the algorithm's accuracy is superior to LSQ, and vice versa.

Fig. 15 compares metaheuristic algorithms with LSQ by presenting results for this performance indicator. It can be observed that metaheuristic algorithms perform drastically better for the frequency domain measurements compared to the time domain. Such a characteristic is particularly evident for FA. An hypothesis for this phenomenon is as follows: Calculation times for LSQ (until convergence) are generally at least an order of magnitude lower than for the metaheuristic algorithms, which makes it computationally far more efficient. LSQ's Achilles' heel is that it is prone to getting stuck in local optima, especially for more complex models, where metaheuristic algorithms are playing out their advantages.

Metaheuristic algorithms present significant advantages over LSQ for all frequency domain measurements. In particular, this can be observed in FA, GWO, PSO and WOA. The advantage tends to increase for rising model complexity. However, LSQ is the most appropriate method for time-domain measurements, even if GWO and PSO may provide comparable accuracy. In summary, both GWO and PSO are suitable algorithms with outstanding generalization performance in uncertain measurement environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of battery model parameters on SOH and SOC
ce:section.ce:para: While parameterizing a battery model, it is imperative to confirm that all parameters consistently have a valid physical meaning. This is ensured in this work by predefining upper and lower bounds for each parameter to be identified. However, further to this, evaluating the dependence of the individual parameters on SOH and SOC remains a critical metric to differentiate the strengths and weaknesses of the algorithms. Effective and low-noise identification of each parameter's trend ensures the enhanced robustness in practical applications.

In this work, all the parameters mentioned in Section 2.1 are analyzed separately. The ohmic impedance
R 0
of the frequency domain model as well as the time domain model are utilized as a proxy to discuss the ability of each algorithm. They are depicted in Fig. 16 and Fig. 17 , respectively. Due to the excessive amount of data, figures corresponding to other parameters are placed in Appendix B. That is, the trends of the frequency domain model parameters are displayed in Figs. B.1 through B.7, while Figs. B.8 through B.10 illustrate the corresponding trends of the time domain model.

As can be observed in Fig. 16, the variation of
R 0
due to different SOCs is evident in the frequency domain model. It is readily apparent for all models that
R 0
decreases with increasing SOC. The rise of
R 0
with decreasing SOH is likewise a predictable trend, that is universally observed for all combinations of algorithms and models. Another noteworthy phenomenon is that as the complexity of the model grows, various algorithms more frequently present values near the upper and lower bounds. This implies that algorithms where this phenomenon is more pronounced will be more sensitive to the chosen settings for the upper and lower bounds. This also means that it will be more challenging to get such algorithms to work stably.

Such a difference in stability is illustrated more clearly in the trends of
R ct
and L (Fig. B.2 and Fig. B.7). Theoretically both parameters should exhibit a downward trend with decreasing SOH. During the actual identification, FA and PSO show consistent and excellent ability to track the parameter trend, while WOA does so only in most of the cases. The excessive outliers obtained by the rest of the algorithms may not significantly affect the accuracy (i.e., RMSE) of the model, but such reduction in the correlation between the parameters and SOH/SOC can affect the usefulness of the battery state estimation.

For the time-domain model, the trend of
R 0
as a representative can be visualized as illustrated in Fig. 17. However, unlike the frequency domain model, the difference in stability between various algorithms is already visible in that figure alone. For example, FA results are considerably more noisy than those from PSO. This characterization is compounded in Figs. B.8 through B.10. The increase in outliers makes most of the parameters obtained using FA and GA unusable when estimating the battery state. PSO and GWO, on the other hand, maintain low outlier levels. Clearly identifiable trends in parameter variations can always be observed when applying these latter two algorithms. However, the large performance gaps between different algorithms make the correct choice of algorithm and the appropriate setting of upper and lower bounds mandatory when identifying time domain models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of SOH estimation accuracy based on ECM parameters
ce:section.ce:para: ECM parameters are critical to the efficient estimation of battery states. In this section, a SOH estimation example is provided to discuss the effect of various algorithms on state estimation accuracy. SOH estimation is achieved by a back propagation neural network optimized by a genetic algorithm (GA-BPNN), which is reported in [53]. The model inputs are ECM parameters and the output is battery SOH. The training set cells are employed to train the BPNN and the test set cells are utilized for model accuracy validation. Fig. 18 demonstrates the SOH estimation accuracy under various conditions. In order to effectively demonstrate the effect of SOC on SOH estimation, the inputs are selected as ECM parameters from three SOC ranges, i.e. high, medium and low, respectively.

A striking characteristic is that the accuracy of ECM parameter identification and SOH estimation accuracy are remarkably positively correlated. Consequently, FA, PSO and GWO consistently provide excellent SOH estimates based on frequency domain measurements. Moreover, LSQ, PSO and GWO demonstrate a significant advantage in the utilization of time-domain measurement data. Although usually enhanced ECM identification accuracy is achievable by employing more complex models (Cf. Fig. 12), SOH estimates do not completely reflect this. For time-domain measurements, a more complex hysteresis model leads to improved SOH estimates. However, in the frequency domain measurement environment, the simplest model can instead accomplish the most effective SOH estimation. A possible explanation could be that more complex models provide a more satisfactory fit to the measurement data. Nevertheless, in case this is primarily an overfitting effect, the model will not reveal more details about the actual state (e.g. the SOH) of the battery.

Overall, different combinations of algorithms and models considerably influence SOH estimation performance. The relative different between the optimal and the poorest solution reaches 80 %. This clearly demonstrates the importance of selecting an appropriate combination of model and parameter identification algorithm.

In this paper, 14 lithium-ion batteries were employed for cyclic aging tests. 615 EIS measurements and 2255 HPPC measurements were performed at SOH between 80 % and 100 %. Within the scope of commonly employed algorithms, 6 metaheuristics and 1 least-squares-based method were implemented to identify ECM parameters based on the above measurements. 5 FOMs and 2 RC models are included in the discussion. 66,295 parameter identifications provide the basis for a comprehensive discussion of the performance of various parameter identification methods from a variety of perspectives. The key findings include:
1.
Each identification algorithm performs differently when confronted with frequency-domain and time-domain measurements. The results demonstrate that LSQ, as a popular algorithm, exhibits inferior performance than metaheuristic algorithms when identifying frequency domain models. However, LSQ is the most accurate option when identifying time domain models. FA, in contrast, performs almost complementary to LSQ. PSO and GWO are the most desirable approaches when BMS encounters uncertain measurement environments. They are comparable to FA in the frequency domain and provide approximately the equivalent accuracy to LSQ in the time domain.
2.
SOH and SOC can influence the performance of parameter identification algorithms. Nevertheless, the relative benefits and drawbacks of distinct algorithms do not fluctuate with shifts in SOH or SOC. Therefore, modifying the parameter identification algorithm with respect to battery state is not essential. PSO, GWO and LSQ are appropriate for parameter identification irrespective of the application.
3.
In practical applications the computational duration should be carefully selected. To improve metaheuristic algorithms' applicability, excessively extended durations are inherently undesirable. Therefore, accuracy at acceptable durations is an essential perspective to examine the algorithms. PSO and FA achieved the most efficient convergence rate in this work. An acceptable duration of 3 s is also feasible for GWO and WOA.
4.
Although model accuracy improves progressively with complexity, this does not guarantee that SOH estimation based on complex models will provide more accurate estimates. On the contrary, when employing appropriate methods such as PSO, GWO, or LSQ, simplified model structures facilitate the monitoring of the battery state more efficiently.

Based on the analysis above, PSO and GWO fit ECM parameters most accurately in complex (EIS) measurement environments. In contrast, LSQ exhibits comparable capability in estimating battery aging states for more simple (HPPC) setups.

All results in this paper are derived from measurements of NCA cells at 25 °C. Both the temperature and the batteries' electrochemical properties have the potential to affect the performance of parameter identification algorithms. Therefore, the applicability of these conclusions to various temperatures and electrochemical properties will be the focus of our subsequent studies. The performance of various parameter identification algorithms on real vehicle data is also a worthwhile area for further research.

Jinghua Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yixin Liu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Josef Kainz: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by the Bavarian State Ministry of Science, Research and Art (Grant number: H.2-F1116.WE/52/2). This work is also supported by Vitesco Technologies Group AG.

Robustness of individual parameter identification algorithms to distinct models at varying SOH: based on measured data from cell #10 at 70 % SOC.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison, selection, and parameterization of electrical battery models for automotive applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on battery management system from the modeling efforts to its multiapplication and integration
